content.text: System Cards with Operational Context and Limits

A system card extends the model card concept by documenting the full operational context in which Claude runs: the system prompt, integration architecture, runtime constraints, and governance layer. Where a model card answers 'what is this AI and what is it for?', a system card answers 'exactly how is this AI configured, constrained, and monitored in production?'

System cards are the primary evidence artifact for demonstrating responsible deployment to both internal stakeholders and external reviewers. They are distinct from Anthropic's own model cards (which document the base model) and live closer to the application layer — one system card per Claude integration, not per base model.
The same base Claude model can be deployed safely or unsafely depending entirely on the operator-controlled layers. A system card that documents these layers serves three purposes:
Auditability: an auditor can confirm that the system prompt enforces the stated restrictions without testing the live system
Accountability: the AI owner's attestation on the system card creates a named responsible party for each configuration decision
Change control: when the system prompt changes, the system card delta shows exactly what changed and who approved it

The EU AI Act (Art. 13) requires that users be provided 'sufficient information' about the AI system's capabilities and limitations. A system card — in simplified form — directly satisfies this transparency requirement.

The most important section of a system card is the operator configuration: the system prompt, permitted and prohibited topics, persona settings, and output format constraints. This section must be specific enough that an auditor or red-teamer can replicate the operational behavior.

# system-card.yaml — Layer 2: Operator Configuration
operator_config:
  system_prompt_hash: "sha256:a3f7c2..."   # hash of prod system prompt; full text in vault
  system_prompt_summary: >
    Claude is configured as a customer support assistant for ExampleCorp's
    software products. It may answer questions about product features,
    subscription plans, and known bugs. It must not provide legal, medical,
    or financial advice. It must not discuss competitor products by name.
    It must identify itself as an AI assistant if asked directly.

  permitted_topics:
    - "ExampleCorp product features and documentation"
    - "Subscription management and billing questions"
    - "Known bug status and workaround guidance"
    - "General software troubleshooting"

  prohibited_topics:
    - "Legal advice or contract interpretation"
    - "Medical advice"
    - "Competitor product comparisons"
    - "Internal ExampleCorp employee matters"
    - "Speculation about unannounced features"

  persona:
    name: "Aria"
    tone: "professional and friendly"
    must_disclose_ai_identity: true
    may_deny_ai_identity: false   # EU AI Act Art. 50 compliance

  output_format:
    max_response_length_tokens: 800
    preferred_format: "prose paragraphs; bullet lists for step-by-step instructions"
    language: "English; respond in user's language if detected"

The integration layer documents how the application builds API calls, what data is injected into the context window, and how conversation history is managed. This layer is often underdocumented — engineers know it intuitively but it is never written down for auditors or new team members.

# system-card.yaml — Layer 3: Integration Context
integration_context:
  api_call_pattern: "single-turn: one messages.create() per support ticket"
  context_window_contents:
    - "system prompt (static)"
    - "ticket body text (user-provided, may contain PII)"
    - "product category tag (injected from ticketing system)"
    - "agent context notes (optional, written by human agent)"

  data_NOT_in_context:
    - "customer account history"
    - "previous ticket conversations"
    - "customer PII beyond what is in the ticket body"

  tool_use: false   # Claude does not have tool access in this integration
  conversation_history_retained: false   # single-turn; no multi-turn memory

  pii_handling:
    before_api_call: "PII minimizer strips SSNs, phone numbers, and email
                      addresses from ticket body; replaces with [PII-REDACTED]"
    after_response: "Response is stored in ticketing system; not logged to
                     AI evidence pipeline in plaintext"

  error_handling:
    on_5xx_from_anthropic: "return 'draft unavailable' to agent UI; no retry"
    on_timeout: "10-second timeout; same fallback as 5xx"
    on_content_policy_block: "log event to incident queue; alert Tier-2 support"

Runtime constraints are the technical guardrails that enforce the operator configuration at the code level. Documenting them in the system card allows auditors to verify that stated restrictions are actually enforced, not just declared:

# system-card.yaml — Layer 4: Runtime Constraints
runtime_constraints:
  model_version_pinned: "claude-opus-4-8"
  temperature: 0.3    # low variance for consistent customer-facing output
  max_tokens: 800     # enforced; truncated responses trigger retry with shorter prompt
  top_p: null         # not set; using temperature only

  token_budget_per_request:
    input_limit: 4000
    output_limit: 800
    enforcement: "hard limit; request rejected if estimated input > 4000 tokens"

  output_filtering_layer:
    enabled: true
    filter_type: "regex + keyword blocklist"
    blocked_patterns:
      - "competitor product names (list in secrets manager)"
      - "explicit pricing commitments ('we guarantee', 'you will pay')"
    on_filter_match: "response blocked; agent receives 'content policy' message"

  logging:
    what_is_logged: "request_id, prompt_sha256, model, token_usage, stop_reason"
    what_is_NOT_logged: "prompt plaintext, response plaintext"
    log_retention_days: 90
    log_destination: "CloudWatch → S3 evidence bucket (encrypted at rest)"

  human_review_gate:
    type: "mandatory"
    mechanism: "draft displayed in agent UI; agent must click 'Send' explicitly"
    bypass_possible: false
The governance layer ties the system card to the organization's compliance program. It answers 'who is responsible for this system?' and 'what oversight mechanisms apply?':

# system-card.yaml — Layer 5: Governance Context
governance:
  ai_owner: "cx-platform@example.com"
  ai_owner_manager: "vp-cx@example.com"
  business_unit: "Customer Experience Platform"

  applicable_regulations:
    - regulation: "CCPA/CPRA"
      reason: "ticket inputs may contain California consumer PII"
      obligation: "opt-out mechanism; data minimization"
    - regulation: "EU AI Act Art. 50"
      reason: "chatbot interacts with natural persons (if EU customers contact support)"
      obligation: "must disclose AI identity; done via persona config"
    - regulation: "SOC 2 CC6/CC9"
      reason: "part of SOC 2 scope for customer data platform"
      obligation: "API key rotation; output filtering; logging"

  review_cadence:
    quarterly_attestation: true
    ai_owner_attests_by: "15th of quarter-end month"
    annual_red_team: true
    next_red_team_date: "2026-10-01"

  incident_escalation:
    first_responder: "on-call-sre@example.com"
    compliance_escalation: "compliance@example.com"
    legal_escalation: "legal@example.com"

  change_control:
    system_prompt_changes: "require PR approval from AI Owner + Security team"
    model_version_changes: "require change ticket + re-run of evaluation suite"
    runtime_config_changes: "require PR approval from AI Owner"

A system card that is out of date is an active liability: it misrepresents the system's behavior to auditors and creates discrepancies that regulators will treat as evidence of governance failures. Establish automated checks that flag when the card may be stale:

# system_card_staleness_check.py
# Detects when the live configuration has diverged from the documented system card

import hashlib, json, boto3
from pathlib import Path

sm = boto3.client("secretsmanager")

def get_live_system_prompt_hash(secret_name: str) -> str:
    prompt = sm.get_secret_value(SecretId=secret_name)["SecretString"]
    return "sha256:" + hashlib.sha256(prompt.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

def check_system_card_current(card_path: str, prompt_secret: str) -> dict:
    card = json.loads(Path(card_path).read_text())
    documented_hash = card.get("operator_config", {}).get("system_prompt_hash", "")
    live_hash = get_live_system_prompt_hash(prompt_secret)

    result = {
        "card_path": card_path,
        "documented_hash": documented_hash,
        "live_hash": live_hash,
        "in_sync": documented_hash == live_hash,
        "last_review_date": card.get("last_review_date"),
    }
    if not result["in_sync"]:
        print(f"SYSTEM CARD DRIFT: prompt hash mismatch in {card_path}")
        print(f"  Documented: {documented_hash}")
        print(f"  Live:       {live_hash}")
        print("  Update the system card and re-attest.")
    else:
        print(f"System card in sync: {card_path}")
    return result

if __name__ == "__main__":
    check_system_card_current(
        card_path="system-card.json",
        prompt_secret="prod/claude/customer-support-system-prompt",
    )
Differences Between System Cards Across Use Cases

Different Claude integrations within the same organization should each have their own system card. The governance layer can point to shared policies, but the operator configuration and runtime constraints must accurately reflect each specific deployment:
Salary comparisons, legal advice, disciplinary history
NYC LL144 (if used in hiring), GDPR (EU employees)

Maintaining separate system cards per integration protects against scope creep: an engineer cannot justify using the customer support Claude integration for an HR use case if the system card explicitly excludes it.
Red-Team Testing Documentation in System Cards

A system card that only documents intended behavior without evidence of adversarial testing provides incomplete assurance. Red-team findings and their mitigations belong in the system card alongside normal evaluation results:

# system-card.yaml — Red Team Section
red_team:
  last_conducted: "2026-01-08"
  conducted_by: "Internal Security team + external red-team vendor"
  scope: "System prompt bypass attempts, topic restriction circumvention,
          persona manipulation, PII extraction via prompt injection"

  findings:
    - id: "RT-001"
      severity: "medium"
      description: "Indirect prompt injection via ticket body could instruct
                    Claude to ignore topic restrictions in certain phrasing patterns"
      status: "mitigated"
      mitigation: "Added explicit injection-resistance instruction to system prompt;
                   input sanitization layer strips common injection patterns"
      mitigated_date: "2026-01-20"

    - id: "RT-002"
      severity: "low"
      description: "Claude would occasionally speculate about unreleased features
                    when phrasing was sufficiently indirect"
      status: "mitigated"
      mitigation: "Added explicit 'do not speculate about unreleased products'
                   instruction and example in system prompt"
      mitigated_date: "2026-01-18"

    - id: "RT-003"
      severity: "low"
      description: "Claude might disclose the existence of the system prompt
                    if directly and persistently asked"
      status: "accepted"
      rationale: "Disclosure of system prompt existence (not content) is not a
                  material risk for this use case; EU AI Act Art. 50 disclosure
                  obligation is about AI identity, not system prompt content"
      risk_accepted_by: "cx-platform@example.com"
      risk_accepted_date: "2026-01-22"

  next_red_team: "2026-10-01"
  frequency: "annual minimum; additionally after major system prompt changes"
Linking System Cards to Incident Records

When a Claude-related incident occurs, the system card is the first reference document: it defines what the system was supposed to do, making deviations immediately visible. The incident record should reference the system card version that was active at the time of the incident:

# incident_system_card_link.py — links incident records to system card snapshots

import json, datetime, hashlib, boto3
from pathlib import Path

s3 = boto3.client("s3")
EVIDENCE_BUCKET = "my-company-compliance-evidence"

def snapshot_system_card_for_incident(
    incident_id: str,
    card_path: str,
) -> str:
    """
    Takes an immutable snapshot of the current system card at incident time.
    Returns the S3 key of the snapshot — reference this from the incident record.
    """
    card_content = Path(card_path).read_bytes()
    sha256 = hashlib.sha256(card_content).hexdigest()
    
    key = (
        f"incidents/{incident_id}/system-card-snapshot-{sha256[:12]}.yaml"
    )
    s3.put_object(
        Bucket=EVIDENCE_BUCKET,
        Key=key,
        Body=card_content,
        ContentType="application/x-yaml",
        ServerSideEncryption="aws:kms",
    )
    print(f"System card snapshot for {incident_id}: s3://{EVIDENCE_BUCKET}/{key}")
    return key

def link_incident_to_card(incident_record: dict, card_path: str) -> dict:
    snapshot_key = snapshot_system_card_for_incident(
        incident_record["id"], card_path
    )
    incident_record["system_card_at_incident_time"] = snapshot_key
    incident_record["system_card_sha256"] = hashlib.sha256(
        Path(card_path).read_bytes()
    ).hexdigest()
    return incident_record

As the number of Claude integrations grows, maintaining an inventory of all active system cards becomes a compliance requirement in its own right. An organization that cannot enumerate its AI systems cannot demonstrate they are all governed:

The system card registry is the authoritative answer to 'what AI systems does this organization operate?' — auditors, regulators, and the board AI committee will all ask this question. Maintain it as a single source of truth, not as spreadsheets in individual team folders.

System cards document the full operational context of a Claude deployment across five layers: the base model (Anthropic's responsibility), the operator configuration (system prompt, permitted and prohibited topics, persona), the integration context (what data enters the context window and how), the runtime constraints (token limits, output filtering, logging), and the governance layer (owner, regulations, review cadence). Each layer must be documented specifically enough that an auditor can verify the system behaves as stated. Automate staleness detection by hashing the live system prompt and comparing it to the documented hash at each deployment; drift between the live system and the card is a compliance gap, not just a documentation problem.
